speaker: interviewee
text: One is 25, and he's Joshua. And then the younger one is only 10, Davida. She was named after my husband. And the youngest is Esther, And she was just nine years old in July.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all live in Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do they come to visit often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, quite often. And I go into Chicago, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you like the weather in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't live there anymore. And as much as I don't care too much for the summers in Florida, I don't think I could live in Chicago anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with the winters, with the snow, with the sleet, with all that. I'm not used to it anymore. So while I lived there, it was OK. I didn't know any different. But now that I got used to the Florida weather, it's-- I couldn't live there anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do the grandchildren enjoy the Florida weather when they come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when they come, they love it. They sit in the pool, they go to the ocean. They enjoy it, because we're only three miles from the ocean here, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is September 5th, 1995. Survivor Tema Lichtenstein, maiden name Zylbersztejn. Interviewer, Elisa Arden. City, Deerfield Beach, Florida. Country, United States of America. And the language is English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Tape one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is September 5th, 1995. My name is Elisa Arden, A-R-D-E-N. I am conducting an interview with survivor Tema Lichtenstein, L-I-C-H-T-E-N-S-T-E-I-N. Maiden name, Zylbersztejn, Z-Y-L-B-E-R-S-Z-T-E-J-N. The location is Deerfield Beach, Florida, United States of America. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Tema Lichtenstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-C-H-T-E-N-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zylbersztejn. Z-Y-L-B-E-R-S-Z-T-E-J-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I changed my name after I escaped from camp. I was on Aryan papers, which-- I didn't have them, but I was going as an Aryan. And my name was Teresa Smiechowska of Bratislava, Smiechowska, both names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last name? S-M-I-E-C-H-O-W-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 8, 1918.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell me something about the town that you were born in and grew up in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, we left Warsaw-- I was a young girl. And I really don't recall except playing with the kids and going to grade school. Otherwise, I was not involved in anything more. And then in 1931 or '32, we moved to a town about 120 kilometers north of Warsaw, and that's when I attended school.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the name of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plozk. P-L-O-Z-K.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was there a large Jewish community in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the whole population before the war was about 36,000. And I would say about a third of them all-- about a third was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell me about your father. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Jacob [INAUDIBLE] Zylbersztejn. And he was a cabinet-maker. Handmade furniture only.

speaker: interviewee
text: And was your father very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very. I think my mother was more religious than my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was easier to adapt to situations, it seems. Her name was Frajda Zylbersztejn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And do you remember observing the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Every holiday was observed, yes. We were a very traditional family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who else was in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were five children. I have an older brother and sister, and a younger brother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And could you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The oldest one was Ajcyk Izchak. The-- my sister's name was Eda. And of course I'm next, Tema. And then was Stefan, which changed his name to Sholom. And [? Felicia, ?] who became Zipora in Israel now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did your family belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. Not really. They attended synagogue on high holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did your father have any special interests or hobbies, other than his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't have too much time for hobbies, raising a family of five and being the only breadwinner. He-- I don't think he had time for hobbies, really. But he belonged-- he was a Zionist and belonged to the Zionist organization. That he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: What were the family's feelings about Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very much involved. We were all raised that way. But there was even a time when my parents considered moving there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in 1930, '31. And-- but we didn't have enough money to buy tickets for seven people, so we had to stay in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: What language did you speak in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my home, mostly Yiddish. Mostly. And then when we started school, of course, we spoke mostly Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you remember the school that you attended as a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a young child, it was a-- a grade school not too far from where I lived in Warsaw. And I don't remember the name of the street, [INAUDIBLE]. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's where-- you know, grade school only. And then, of course, after we moved to Plozk, I went to the gymnasium, or high school, the way it's called here. We called it gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were there non-Jewish children in your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the grade school only, but not in the high school. It was a Jewish high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you or your family have much interaction with non-Jewish people or children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not an awful lot, except neighbors. I would say mostly neighbors. I remember, though, I remember one year that we invited some friends-- non-Jewish friends-- for the Seder at Passover. But otherwise, no, not really. Not too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you aware of any anti-semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I felt it. We felt it almost daily. And as a matter of fact, my-- my husband as a young man, we had a place in Plozk what they called [INAUDIBLE]. It was on a high mountain overlooking the Vistula River. And we were walking back and forth or sitting on the benches and talking, and somebody stabbed him with a knife inside, through his winter coat. He carried a scar for the rest of his life on his back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was anything said?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we felt it. We really felt it all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did they say anything when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just did it and ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was your family aware of what was going on in Germany in the '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- oh, after Hitler came to power? Yeah, I remember. I'll never forget my mother reading-- reading the morning papers and crying her eyes out because of what went on, especially when the Jews, when they started transporting them into Poland. The-- the Polish citizens, so to speak, the ones what were not born in Germany, and they-- and they brought them by train to [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and I'll never forget my mother's-- constant. Every morning, she opened the paper and she was crying. We were quite aware of what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to these Jews that came from Germany? Did any come to your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to my town. My town, we didn't have any.

speaker: interviewee
text: What were your activities when you were a school girl? Did you join any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged to [NON-ENGLISH] and-- and to [INAUDIBLE], which was a social group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's about it. And schoolwork.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are these Zionist groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the [INAUDIBLE]? Yes. That was a Zionist group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you interested in going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, when I was a little older, I think I would have gone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Probably would have gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: What were your goals as a young girl growing up in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: For your future.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God, I don't know. Did I give it much thought? I-- I have to think about it, really, whether I gave it much thought at the time. We were so busy with everyday life, with-- with meeting ends and-- and all that that, that as a young-- a young girl, we didn't think about it and didn't worry about it too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it difficult for your father to make a living then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very difficult. Very difficult. I mean, it was a private school and he had to pay tuition. And sometimes it was not enough. But somehow he managed, kept us all in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: After school, where you able to work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did work shortly before the war. I went to Warsaw. And that-- the reason for this was because they-- they revoked the-- the right to have the school open. So most of the students that were in school then were left without the school, without anything to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Warsaw and I got a job. I applied also to a state school-- state high school. And at first, they said yes, they would have room in my grade, and yes I can apply. Now to apply, at the time, was 15 zlotys, which was an awful lot of money. A family could almost live a whole week on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but then when I filled out the papers and they saw my last name, Zylbersztejn, there was no doubt that I couldn't be anything else but Jewish. And they said well, we'll try. When they said, we'll try, and they didn't have a place for sure, I wasn't going to spend the 15-- 15 zlotys. It was too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and that's when I went to Warsaw and I got a job. And I worked in what we called the perfume area. And I had a sister living in Warsaw, so when the war broke out, that's where I was working and that's where I was stuck, in Warsaw. And I couldn't-- couldn't find my-- my way to go back to Plozk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you have any other close relatives that lived near you as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very far, but not too close. Not in the same city. My-- my mother's parents and family, my father's parents and brothers and sisters. But not in Warsaw. They did not live in Warsaw, nor did they live in Plozk later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: What were your grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my grandfather on my father's side, my paternal, was Lazar. And as a matter of fact, my oldest grandson is named after him, Joshua Lazar. And my-- my grandmother's name was Golda. On my mother's side, it was Avrum Hyam, and my grandmother's name was Pesa. And they, of course, lived in two different cities in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: You were telling us that you were working in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you there when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bombing? Oh, yes, I lived through it in Warsaw. Mm-hm. Yes. It was-- it was-- the only thing we could find to eat were-- were herring and pickles, and no water. I remember taking, once, a bucket, and I went to the Vistula to get some water. And a German noticed me, and he pulled the bucket for me and poured out the water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we had-- we had no water at all, and food was very scarce. Only what somebody, you know, could save and head home. But in the streets, you could not buy anything besides herring and pickles.

speaker: interviewee
text: How long after the start of the war did it take the Germans to arrive in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were coming in almost immediately. I mean, it didn't take long at all. I would say in two days they were all over Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you able to contact your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until after the war. There was no way of contacting them during the war. But after the war, there was the first bus that was leaving from Warsaw to Plozk. And one of my friends went there, and I remember I had my pay for the last week of work. So I asked her to please bring it to my parents and tell them that I'm alive. And I had to wait much later for a horse and-- and wagon to travel from Warsaw to Plozk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So how long did you have to wait, then, until you'd go to Plozk, from when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't come to Plozk till about the middle of October, I would say. And the war, of course, broke out the first of September.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened when you got to Plozk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I got to Plozk, I-- of course, my parents were there, and just my younger sister. The two brothers and the sister that lived in Warsaw, they were on their way to Russia already to escape Hitler. So I didn't see them until after the war ended. But I was with my younger sister. We were, later on, together and we went through camps together and the ghettos together and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did you feel when the Germans came into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Truthfully, if I didn't know what went on with the Jews that they sent back to Poland, I wouldn't know what to expect, because-- but-- but this was so real that we were all scared, and we were talking about it. We were afraid that the young men should run away. They should go to Russia. We didn't know that the old people and the children and the girls were in just as much, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, danger as the young men were.

speaker: interviewee
text: When did laws against the Jews start?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost immediately. Almost immediately. I came home-- they formed the ghetto in 19-- the end of 1940, I think. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I lost my train of thought. What was the question?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. You were-- I was asking you about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when things started to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Happen to the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, almost-- almost immediately. And I was walking-- I remember one incident, I was walking the street with my younger sister on the sidewalk, and a German came towards us and he stopped us. And he said, wo gehst du? Where are you going? And I said, I live here. And he looked at us-- we were both blond and blue eyes, and they couldn't stand it. So he took another look at us, and he said, [GERMAN]. That means, get going, you know. Just disappear like-- you know. And he let us go. But that was in the very beginning. Those things did not happen later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was there a registration of Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Golly. I'm not so sure. I am not sure. The Juden-- no, there was not a Judenrat yet, but they would have a complete list of all the Jews. But I don't think that there was a regis--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is this what the Germans used then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They probably used that. I-- I can't say for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you say that a ghetto was formed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was there a wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But there were guards, and there were two points where we could-- actually, nobody was allowed to leave, but for the Germans to come in and out more. And for the Polish police, that they could come into the ghetto. But otherwise, we were not allowed to go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: What nationality were the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, German. That was before the war brought in the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was your father able to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he worked. He worked for the-- for the Germans, because when they found out that he's such a fine cabinet-maker, furniture-maker, they wanted him to make some things for them, which he did. And they were so happy with him-- so pleased-- that one German told him, don't worry, you'll be the last Jew to be killed. In other words, he didn't leave him any hope right away in the beginning that he's going to save him. He just assured him that he'll be the last Jew to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: What about food? Where you able to have enough food?

speaker: interviewee
text: While-- while in the ghetto, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plozk. Yes, at first it wasn't too bad. At first, we-- we could find food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you able to take all your belongings with you when you went into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they put in three, four families into maybe a three-room apartment. And just bare necessities, maybe pots and pans and a bed and some linens. And there was no room for anything else. And-- and we were my parents, my younger sister and I. The rest, as I said, was in Russia. So the-- the four of us were in that one room, and that's all we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did the Germans distribute documents? Did you have identity cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to the Jews. No. The Poles had Kennkarte, they called it. They-- they had it, which later on I-- I had one myself with my picture in it. But when the time came they took us into camp, my sister didn't have it. And I didn't want to leave her alone so I had to tear up my-- my picture so they wouldn't identify me, and I dug a little hole in the ground with my shoe and I threw in the tiny pieces of my picture and covered it up and stayed in camp with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you have to wear the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in camp. There was nobody else but us. But in the-- in the ghetto in Plozk, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you ever try to leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you able to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. Yes, I did. Not that I was able to. I-- I-- I got out, and I was not easily recognized, and I could do it. I could do it. And I went-- I remember I went to the drugstore to get some medicine for my former teacher's child that took very sick. And she came and actually asked me to do it for her. And I did, and I brought the medication to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: What would have happened if you had been caught outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Death, immediately. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You were caught on the train or anywhere else-- anywhere else outside the ghetto, you were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was everyone given some sort of work to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, forced work, sure. They were catching people on-- from their-- from their homes, from the streets. I would say mostly men. I don't remember them taking any girls that way to work. And they were-- they really didn't have much work for them, but they beat them up. When they came at the end of the day from work, the family couldn't recognize them. And that-- that was going on constantly, every single day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you taken for work at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I was not taken for work to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: What about the young children? Did they have schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with the young children started when they made [? Plozk, ?] what they said Judenrein. In other words, no Jews remained. There were some people that came out with children. Some came out with infants that they-- they put something around their neck in [INAUDIBLE] to hold up the child in it. The German would pull it off their neck and throw the child against a tree, against anything that was there. That was right away in '41. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So no children. And the younger children, when they put us on-- on open trucks, the little children couldn't-- we were pushed so tightly that the little children could not catch their breath, and they arrived at the destination death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did this happen while you were in the ghetto, or when you left the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after-- after they sent us out from Plozk. They took us there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was the entire--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was like a-- a transit place, you know, that everybody was-- they rushed everybody down from that-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] there were broken legs and broken arms, and the German pushed them down and they couldn't make it. And then was a little opening, like a little door, and the SS men were standing on both sides in lines with canes. And there wasn't a single-- we all had to go through that gate, the little gate. So there wasn't a single one that wasn't hit. One of my girlfriends that-- she actually bore my same maiden-- same maiden name as I did, they hit us so hard in the back of the head that, in no time, she started vomiting and she died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is while you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In that transit camp, sort of.

speaker: interviewee
text: Transit camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mm-hm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was hit, and I ran after him like helping him. And the SS man ran after us, you know. And my father said, get away, get away, you'll get hit, too. And they kept us there about two-- two to three days. And then they put us on trains and took us south to Poland. And that's where I wound out in Bodzyntyn. That's near Kielce.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, Bodzyntyn? B-O-D-Z-Y-N-T-Y-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what kind of a place was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very small little town. Well, more than a village, but a very small town with-- I-- I don't recall what the population of Jews was there, but we were-- they were already-- they were not in a ghetto yet, no. Uh-uh. But we were put wherever with a family, wherever they could find a place for us. That's-- they separated families, and so on. I was separated from my parents, because my parents were sent to a different town on a different day, which I later-- later on I found out and I caught up with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was your sister with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was with my parents. No, I was all alone.
